segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with sweeping aerial views of Shanghai’s iconic skyline, showcasing landmarks like the Oriental Pearl Tower, Shanghai Tower, and the Shanghai World Financial Center, with the Huangpu River winding through the city, all set to upbeat, slightly exotic electronic background music. The scene transitions to a woman in a brown tank top and patterned skirt dancing joyfully on a sunlit sidewalk, followed by a panoramic city shot with 'SHANGHAI' superimposed. A male narrator introduces the segment, stating, "Hey guys, and welcome to Cool Vision. In this video, we're going to visit Shanghai." A man in a pink shirt then appears on the Bund, gesturing enthusiastically toward the skyline, as a second, more enthusiastic male voice exclaims, "Welcome to Shanghai, guys! This is the third most populous city in the world and the most populous city in China with a population of 26 million," aligning with the 'Population: 26.3 million' text overlay. The video shifts to a 3D animated globe zooming into China, highlighting Shanghai’s location on the East China Sea, with the Yangtze and Huangpu Rivers labeled, while the first narrator details, "Shanghai's located on China's central coast, bounded to the east by the East China Sea, on the southern estuary of the Yangtze River, with the Huangpu River flowing through it." The map compares Shanghai’s size (6,341 km²) to Moscow and London, with the narrator adding, "The city's area is 6,341 square kilometers, so it's almost three times larger than Moscow and four times larger than London." A woman walks on a rooftop helipad overlooking the city, displaying a 'GDP: $670 billion' text, as the narrator explains, "Today, it's the business capital of China with a GDP of $670 billion, the highest among Chinese cities." This is followed by a massive port filled with shipping containers and cranes, which the narrator identifies as "the world's busiest container port, the Port of Shanghai." The scene cuts to a man in a white shirt eating a pineapple-shaped dish with chopsticks, then to three smiling women walking together on a tree-lined street. The final shot shows a bustling airport terminal with travelers and a prominent 'Shanghai' sign, as the narrator invites viewers to "explore the city, meet locals, try delicious food," before the audio cuts off mid-sentence, "If you arrive at Shanghai Pudong..." The upbeat music continues throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with the sleek white and teal Shanghai Maglev Train arriving and departing at a modern station, with passengers boarding and disembarking. On-screen text highlights its speed of 431 km/h (268 mph) and a price of $7.00, while a speaker explains it's a magnetic levitation train that floats on magnets. He notes its previous cruising speed was 431 km/h, now reduced to 300 km/h, and that it costs 50 Renminbi for an 8-minute journey from the airport to the city, calling it "very convenient and entertaining." Inside, passengers sit calmly in bright turquoise seats as the train rapidly traverses green fields, highways, and urban infrastructure. The scene transitions to an aerial night view of The Bund, illuminated with golden lights along the Huangpu River and crowded with people, as the speaker identifies it as the city's "most popular" and "historic waterfront area," also known as Waitan, explaining that "Bund" means "embankment." A daytime view of the Shanghai skyline, featuring the Oriental Pearl Tower, Shanghai Tower, and Shanghai World Financial Center, is then shown behind red-and-white barriers, with a man in a pink shirt walking towards the camera and gesturing at the impressive cityscape. The video then cuts to a large bronze bull statue in front of classical architecture, which the speaker compares to "the one on Wall Street in New York," as the man in the pink shirt playfully interacts with it, mimicking its pose, with the speaker adding to "grab him by the horns." The speaker continues, describing The Bund as once the "financial heart of the city," now home to "high-end restaurants and hotels" and "1920s era buildings." The scene transitions to a bustling street at dusk, showcasing illuminated colonial-style buildings and traffic, followed by a shot of the Peace Hotel entrance with its iconic green awning and golden signage, as pedestrians walk past. The final frames capture a lively street scene with people crossing the road and walking near the hotel, encapsulating the city’s energetic urban rhythm.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a daytime panoramic view of the grand Art Deco Peace Hotel, completed in 1929, featuring its distinctive green awning and golden signage. The scene transitions to the massive neoclassical HSBC Building, which the speaker identifies as the Hong Kong Shanghai Banking Corporation's branch, as a man in a pink shirt walks and gestures. The camera then pans to The Custom House, a clock-towered building from 1927, which the speaker mentions alongside other historic structures like the Chartered Bank Building, noting that most of these buildings were constructed in the 19th and early 20th centuries by European powers, reflecting a significant European influence. The setting shifts to nighttime, showcasing the illuminated Bund with its ornate colonial-style buildings glowing under streetlights, as pedestrians stroll along the promenade and the Huangpu River reflects the city lights. The speaker then explains the historical context, detailing how foreign powers, primarily Britain, illegally exported opium from India to China since the 18th century, leading to China's desire to stop the trade due to social and economic problems and widespread addiction. Daytime scenes of a busy street with a red double-decker bus and a pedestrian overpass are shown, followed by a red-and-white building overlaid with text about the First Opium War (1839–1842) and a similar building with text about the Second Opium War (1856–1860), illustrating China's loss in these wars and its forced opening to Western traders. The speaker concludes by explaining that Shanghai, from the 1860s to the 1930s, was split into Chinese and European-styled Western halves, becoming a powerful center for foreign establishment. The final shots show the white neoclassical ICBC building and a street lined with classical architecture, bustling with cars and pedestrians, as opera music begins to play.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an upbeat, modern instrumental track accompanying a sweeping aerial view of the Shanghai skyline, showcasing a dense cluster of modern skyscrapers flanking the Huangpu River, with the Pudong and Puxi districts labeled in stylized text. A male narrator explains that the Huangpu River divides the metropolis into these two parts, the East and West sides, and details three ways to cross it: by ferry, car/bus, or the Bund sightseeing tunnel, which costs 50 Renminbi and is mostly for tourists. The scene transitions to a dynamic shot of the Nanpu Bridge, a curved cable-stayed structure bustling with traffic, as vehicles move along its multi-lane roadway. The perspective shifts to a ground-level view from a moving vehicle, capturing the illuminated skyline at dusk, including the iconic Oriental Pearl Tower. The journey continues through a futuristic, blue-lit tunnel, likely part of the Shanghai Maglev Train system, with a price tag of $7.00 displayed. The narrator then introduces Lujiazui, seen in subsequent aerial shots of the financial district highlighting its towering architecture and riverfront position, describing it as a peninsula that was once villages and warehouses but transformed into Shanghai's new financial district, the 'Chinese Wall Street,' since the 1990s due to economic reforms. He adds that 500,000 people work there, with an average age of 29.5, and 70% holding a Master's degree or higher, calling it Shanghai's 'glitzy financial district.' Ground-level scenes follow, showing pedestrians crossing busy intersections under bright sunlight, some holding umbrellas for shade, while modern skyscrapers loom in the background. The camera pans across a roundabout adorned with colorful landscaping and a large decorative sculpture, surrounded by heavy traffic and elevated roadways, all set against the continuous background music, capturing the vibrant, fast-paced urban life of Shanghai.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside a high-speed elevator at the Shanghai Tower, where a digital display shows its ascent at 10.0 m/s, with the floor count decreasing from 99 to 96, and the building's height marked as 460m. The narrator explains this is the world's fastest elevator, originally reaching 20.5 m/s but now slowed to 10 m/s because some people would literally bleed from the nose at higher speeds, accompanied by a whooshing sound. The scene transitions to an outdoor urban plaza where a man in a white polo shirt is seen holding a drone and its remote controller, preparing for flight. He is joined by a woman in a patterned top and another man in a black t-shirt, interacting as he gestures and speaks. The narrator expresses interest in talking to them, noting that flying a drone in Shanghai requires a license, and asks, "Can I ask you what you guys do?" The man in the white polo shirt, identified as a drone operator, responds, "We are doing the building inspection, so you can see the pilots," pointing to himself and another. The narrator confirms they are a "drone inspection company" that provided amazing shots in exchange for "Ramen B." The drone is then shown being assembled on a black case, with close-ups of its propellers and camera. The video cuts to a breathtaking aerial view of the Shanghai skyline, showcasing the Oriental Pearl Tower, Shanghai Tower, and Shanghai World Financial Center, with the Huangpu River flowing below, all captured by the drone. Upbeat, traditional Chinese-sounding music begins as the narrator remarks, "The city is huge." The scene shifts to the interior of the Lujiazui Metro Station, where passengers pass through turnstiles and walk through a modern, brightly lit concourse, with signage for Line 2 and Line 14, and a large digital screen displaying an animated character. The camera moves through the station, then shows the interior of a metro train with passengers seated and standing. The narrator highlights that the "Shanghai Metro is also very huge," describing it as the "longest subway system in the world" with "508 stations, 826 kilometers in length," which is "twice the length of New York City subway." He praises its cleanliness, stating, "Subways are just so clean. So nice." The final scenes depict the station’s commercial area, including a MINISO store, a BIRDIE CUP COFFEE shop with a minimalist design, and a man in a pink shirt walking past, all under bright, clean lighting, as the background music continues.

The voice continues, detailing how to buy and top up a travel card, or use Alipay by scanning a QR code, and mentions local apps like Didi for transport. The voice then recommends Line 2 for hotels. Outdoors, a man in a beige t-shirt and glasses smiles while using a yellow public payphone. He is then seen walking with another man in a black t-shirt, both conversing, as a second male voice speaks in Chinese, then English, saying, "Cool Vision is in town, downtown Shanghai Wukang Road." The first speaker then introduces his friends, Henry and Kunal, noting they both work for Nike, as they pass a Nike store with a large glass facade. The scene concludes inside a modern shopping mall, featuring a vibrant red sculpture of a basketball player mid-dunk, with escalators and shoppers in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in Shanghai's bustling Former French Concession, identified by a yellow text overlay, as a narrator explains that Henry is showcasing his favorite city spots, starting here. Pedestrians, including a man in a striped shirt and a woman in a black dress, cross a zebra crossing under large, leafy trees, while the narrator details the French Concession's history: established in the 1800s after imperialist struggles, existing from 1849-1946 as an upscale residential and retail district and a center of Catholicism. The camera pans along tree-lined streets, revealing vintage-style buildings with arched windows and balconies, some adorned with laundry and red lanterns, as a blue '24路' bus, a red car, and a green taxi pass by amidst the ongoing narration about the area's current popularity with tourists and expats, and Wukang Road's high density of historic villas. The scene transitions to a wider view of the grand, curved brick 'WUKANG MANSION, 1924', where a man in a beige t-shirt and glasses gestures towards the building while speaking to the camera. He explains that the mansion, built in 1924 (making it 100 years old), was originally called "Normandie Apartments" and housed many foreigners, including French and British. The camera zooms in on the mansion’s ornate balconies with flower boxes, then pulls back to show the full structure at a busy intersection with traffic and pedestrians, accompanied by the sounds of city traffic. The final frames show a group of people on scooters, one holding a phone, waiting at a crosswalk as vehicles pass under a clear sky.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an exterior shot of a pale yellow, multi-story building in a quiet, tree-lined neighborhood, where a woman is seen hanging laundry on a balcony. The scene transitions to the entrance of the 'Rose Shanghai Restaurant,' marked by a sign and a decorative chandelier inside, as a male voice explains that 'Henry wanted us to try some local delicacies' and they had a 'fantastic lunch at the Rose Restaurant,' noting that 'Shanghai-nese cuisine is sweet.' Inside, the warmly lit dining area features classic decor, including framed portraits of historical figures on the walls and a checkered floor. The speaker remarks, 'Wow, chandelier is so impressive,' and after a 'Ni hao,' observes that 'it's a busy place.' A group of diners is seated at a round table, enjoying their meal. The camera then focuses on various dishes being served: first, plum pork ribs garnished with green pickles and almonds, which the speaker identifies as 'sweet and sour pork,' explaining it's similar to US sweet and sour chicken but made in a 'very, very complicated way.' Next, shredded jellyfish with green onions is shown. This is followed by shredded lobster buns in chicken sauce, which the speaker refers to as 'chicken sauce, bao,' describing it as 'kind of like a tofu' that makes vegetables and tofu skin stick together. Then, presidential beef with a glossy glaze and green peas is presented, with the speaker admitting it's his 'first time trying this' and questioning why it's called 'President beef.' Finally, a rich crab and shrimp meal is displayed, which the speaker calls 'shrimp with crab meat' and 'another very pleasant' dish. Each dish is presented on a white plate, with on-screen text displaying its name in both Chinese and English, along with the price, all contributing to the cozy and elegant atmosphere enhanced by red curtains, wooden paneling, and soft lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a vibrant, orange-hued dish of stir-fried corn and vegetables, alongside a 'Squirrel mandarin fish' priced at $36.20, served on a white plate in a warmly lit, elegant restaurant interior with red curtains and checkered flooring. A hand uses chopsticks to interact with the fish, revealing its flaky texture. A man in a beige t-shirt and glasses, seated at the table, speaks animatedly, explaining that the 'Squirrel mandarin fish' (Songshu Guiyu in Chinese) is originally from Suzhou but is part of Shanghai cuisine, and is popular with foreigners because it's boneless, unlike most Chinese fish dishes with bones. Another voice briefly responds, "Okay, thank you." The man in beige then states that the check for the meal came to over $200, highlighting that "Shanghai can be pretty expensive." The scene transitions from the restaurant's European-style exterior, featuring potted plants and a red umbrella, to a busy city street with various cars, including a bright blue sports car and a yellow taxi, and pedestrians crossing a zebra crossing. Finally, two men, one in a black t-shirt with an 'ACG' logo and the other the man in the beige t-shirt, walk side by side on a tree-lined sidewalk. A narrator mentions that "Henry and Kunal told me a lot about the local realities," explaining that when inviting a girl on a date in Shanghai, she might ask if your area code is 310, the first three digits of a Shanghai-born person's ID number, which aligns with the subtitles appearing on screen: 'if you Shanghai, you will not accept you, finding a partner,' suggesting a conversation about social integration or relationships in the city.

The scene transitions from the bustling street corner, where people walk past storefronts, into the modern, minimalist coffee shop 'Into the Force'. Inside, patrons are seated at wooden tables amidst unique stacked wooden block walls. A male narrator explains that Shanghai, a city with over 8,000 coffee shops, demands people "move fast and stay caffeinated," leading them to this establishment. A menu board advertises a 'Summer Limited COMBO' with 'EDIBLE CUP™' coffee for ¥50. The focus shifts to two edible cups, one dark marbled and one green-hued, placed on a branded cardboard tray. A man in a beige t-shirt and glasses takes a bite of the brown cup, followed by a man in a black t-shirt biting the green one, both appearing amused and intrigued. Patrons are heard reacting with exclamations like "Yeah, wow!" and a curious "This one?" as chewing sounds fill the air. Another voice playfully asks, "Are you that hungry?" The experience culminates with a delighted "Ooh!" and the surprised comment, "It tastes like a cookie!"

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man in a black t-shirt and blue jeans, who narrates his decision to go cycling around the neighborhood, emphasizing the extreme affordability of shared bikes with a $3 monthly subscription. He scans a QR code on a teal shared bicycle with his smartphone, and the mobile app, which displays a map and a 'Start Riding' button, confirms the unlock with an electronic chime and a female voice saying, 'Hello, hello, Danche.' Transitioning to a first-person perspective, the man begins riding the bike down a picturesque, tree-lined street in the Former French Concession, shaded by large, mature trees with dappled sunlight. He expresses his enjoyment, describing how cycling or walking along these streets, filled with an array of bars, cafes, restaurants, and boutiques, reveals the 'real character of Shanghai' and why it's considered the 'most livable city in China.' As he rides past vintage European-style buildings, including 'RUMORS COFFEE ROASTERS' and a yellow taxi, he continues to narrate, calling cycling the 'best means of transportation' in downtown Shanghai due to no traffic jams and its affordability. Other cyclists and pedestrians, like a woman in a black dress and a man in a striped shirt, are visible. He passes a row of parked shared bikes in various colors and a black sports car, glancing back at the camera with a smile before continuing his ride past a woman in a white dress and a man in a beige t-shirt. The serene, leafy environment is further detailed by a '30' speed limit sign and a blue '泰安路' (Taian Road) street sign. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the man riding away amidst the greenery and historic architecture, as he mentions taking a taxi to another trendy neighborhood after this ride.
